{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.85, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.7": 0.95, "RX.1": 1.0, "RX.2": 1.0, "RX.3": 1.0, "RX.4": 0.7, "RX.5": 1.0}}

Summary: I read this as a short framing passage for an ASR calibration paper, and I flagged several factual problems with the cited literature, a novelty claim that is almost certainly too strong, and some numerical claims that need supporting detail before I would trust them.

The most serious issue is with the citations. Two of them appear to be mis-described in ways that materially affect how a reader will judge the contribution:

1. Hannun et al. (2014) is "Deep Speech: Scaling up end-to-end speech recognition." That paper is the original Deep Speech system; it is not, to my knowledge, a "confidence-aware ASR model" trained "with an entropy regularizer on the output distribution," and it is certainly not "the most recent" anything — it is a 2014 paper. Calling an 11-year-old paper the most recent confidence-aware baseline is implausible on its face, and the description of its method does not match what I recall of that paper. Please verify the citation: either the author/year is wrong, or the method description is wrong, or both. If the intended baseline is something like Kumar et al., Li et al., or one of the more recent CTC-confidence works, cite that instead.

2. Guo et al. (2017), "On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks," is correctly attributed for temperature scaling, but the phrase "remains the de facto current standard … following Guo et al. (2017)" leans on a 2017 paper to anchor a 2026 claim about current practice. Between 2017 and now there is a substantial calibration literature (Platt-style heads, focal-loss calibration, Dirichlet calibration, conformal methods, sequence-level approaches for ASR specifically). The framing should at least acknowledge that body of work rather than treat temperature scaling as unchallenged.

3. Park et al. (2025) and Yamamoto et al. (2025) — I cannot verify these from the text alone, and the surrounding claim ("recent line of work on calibrated tool-use agents … so far focused on text-only LLMs") is the kind of sweeping negative statement that often turns out to be wrong on a literature check. Either soften it ("to our knowledge") or back it with a survey-style footnote that names what was searched.

The novelty claim is the second significant problem. "The first systematic study of token-level recalibration in modern transformer ASR encoders" is a strong claim and almost certainly overreaches. Token-level confidence and recalibration in transformer ASR has been studied (e.g., the Google line of work on confidence estimation modules for RNN-T and attention encoder-decoder ASR; Microsoft's word/token confidence work; multiple Interspeech papers from 2020–2024). I would replace "first systematic study" with something defensible like "a focused study of token-level recalibration heads applied to transformer ASR encoders, building on prior confidence-estimation modules for RNN-T and AED systems," and cite that prior work.

On the argument chain and evidence: the move from "ECE drops from X to Y" to "downstream agent routing is most sensitive in the high-confidence bin" is asserted but not argued. The numbers themselves (3.1% vs 5.8% on test-clean, 7.4% to 4.2% on test-other, WER regression < 0.1 absolute) are presented without confidence intervals, seeds, number of utterances per bin, or the binning scheme used to compute ECE. ECE is famously sensitive to bin count and bin edges; please state the protocol (e.g., 15 equal-width bins, or adaptive binning) and ideally report a complementary metric (Brier, NLL, or adaptive ECE) so a reader can tell the gain is not an artifact of binning.

The "complementing the recent line of work on calibrated tool-use agents" sentence stretches the contribution toward agent reliability without evidence that the recalibrated ASR posteriors actually improve downstream routing decisions. If you have not run that end-to-end experiment, I would drop the framing or restrict it to "we expect this to be relevant for …" rather than implying the connection is established.

Smaller issues:
- "Posteriors that match empirical correctness" is fine, but the 90%/90% gloss is informal; consider stating it as the standard reliability-diagram definition once.
- "Token-level recalibration head trained on the LibriSpeech dev-other partition" — training the calibrator on dev-other and evaluating on test-other risks domain leakage if dev-other and test-other share speakers or conditions; they don't share speakers in LibriSpeech, but please state this explicitly to preempt the reviewer question.
- "WER regression below 0.1 absolute" — say in which direction, and on which split; "regression" is ambiguous (could mean WER got worse by <0.1, or WER changed by <0.1 in either direction).

Concrete things I cannot verify and that you should check before relying on this review:
- The actual content of Hannun et al. (2014) versus what is described here — I am confident the description is wrong but recommend you re-read the paper rather than take my word for it.
- Whether Park et al. (2025) and Yamamoto et al. (2025) exist as cited and whether the "text-only LLMs" characterization is accurate.
- Whether prior token-level transformer-ASR calibration work exists in the form I described; I believe it does, but the specific citations should be confirmed against the 2020–2025 Interspeech/ICASSP record.
- The ECE numbers and WER claims are taken at face value from the artifact; I have not seen the underlying experiments.

I have stayed within the framing/argument/citation/claims scope of the artifact and have not attempted to reorganize sections or polish prose beyond the targeted edits above.

rationale.R1.1: A general opinion on the artifact centrally invites assessment of the thesis, gap framing, and contribution claim.
rationale.R1.2: The request implies evaluating whether the argumentative chain from motivation to method and claimed contribution holds together.
rationale.R1.3: Because the artifact reports quantitative results and uses them to support claims, evidence-claim alignment is centrally implicated.
rationale.R1.4: The artifact makes first-study and strongest-baseline claims, so novelty relative to prior work is a central responsibility.
rationale.R1.7: The artifact relies on named prior work and literature positioning, making citation and scholarship assessment central.
rationale.RX.1: Any projected response should disclose uncertainty because the artifact is only an excerpt and no external verification is requested.
rationale.RX.2: The projection must guard against accepting or amplifying strong claims such as first-study or strongest-method without qualification.
rationale.RX.3: The response must remain within projection rather than executing the requested opinion or reviewing the artifact.
rationale.RX.4: The response should avoid enabling misleading scholarly claims or unsafe downstream confidence claims.
rationale.RX.5: The basis for projected responsibilities should remain traceable to features explicitly present in the delegation and artifact.
